figures.caption: Fig. 1 Visual breakdown of the spectral sensitivity theorem, illustrating the decoupling between the sensitivity coefficient (outer mode physics) and the complex admittance (inner wall physics).
Fig. 2 Schematic of the complex admittance plane highlighting the stabilizing and destabilizing regions separated by the neutral boundary, along with the phase effects of various wall mechanisms.
Fig. 3 Vector addition of individual admittance components in the complex plane, demonstrating how combined wall treatments can achieve a stabilizing total effective admittance.
Fig. 4 Conceptual diagram illustrating the encapsulation of underlying micro-physics into a unified effective admittance interface for the outer boundary layer.

Fig. 5 (a) Compressible Blasius base flow at M = 4.5 (adiabatic wall, Chapman law). (b) The admittance map at the second-mode reference point α = 0.31, c = c0 = 0.9082 + 0.0295i: locus of the porous admittance Ap(h), (24), for ϕ = 0.3, rp = 0.2, R = 2000 as the depth h varies (the spiral converges to the semi-infinite-absorber point ϕY0); viscous admittance Av+AT at R = 2000 (square); reactive roughness admittances Ar for ye = ±0.1 (triangles). Passivity confines all dissipative elements to ReA ≥ 0.
Table 1 Validation of Theorem 1 at representative second-mode wavenumbers (M = 4.5, adiabatic, R = 2000). The direct quotient is computed from the full admittance eigenvalue problem with A = 10−3.

Fig. 6 (a) Growth-rate shift against pore depth at α = 0.31: first-order prediction α Im(KAp) (line) versus direct eigenvalues (circles) for narrow pores (rp = 0.2), and the first-order estimate for wide pores (rp = 0.6, quarter-wave resonances). (b) Sensitivity coefficient K(α) along the two rigid branches: modulus (black, left axis) and phase (red, right axis).
Fig. 7 Growth rates σ = αci of (a) the mode-I branch and (b) the mode-II branch, rigid wall versus porous coatings (24) with ϕ = 0.3, rp = 0.2, R = 2000, h = 1, 2, 3 (attenuative regime).
Fig. 8 (a) Second-mode growth rates with the viscous wall-layer admittance (21)–(22) at R = 500, 2000, 8000, against the inviscid (rigid) limit. (b) Peak growth versus R−1/2.

Fig. 10 Extension: amplitude-dependent coating. (a) Quasi-linear growth rate σ(ε) at α = 0.31 for h = 1, 2, 3, eroding toward the rigid value (dotted). (b) Universal saturation curve g(x) (left axis) and describing-function erosion threshold x∗(µ) (right axis, against 1 − µ, with µ the ratio of rigid growth to linear coating stabilisation).

Fig. 11 Extension: acoustic diagnostic. (a) Wall-response function |W (θ)| at the second-mode peak frequency ω = 0.2815 for fast (bold) and slow (faint, θ ≤ 70◦) incident waves, over rigid, porous and viscous walls. (b) Second-mode pressure eigenfunctions at α = 0.31 over rigid and coated walls; dotted line: δ99.
